nativeRange.habitat: Marshes, pond margins, seasonally flooded wetlands, and slow-moving shallow waters across a broad subtropical to tropical range

summary: Compact grass-like rosette plant from the Americas. Generally considered an easy foreground or carpeting species that spreads by runners to form dense, lawn-like growth.
careNotes: Helanthium tenellum (formerly Echinodorus tenellus) is a small rosulate plant generally regarded as one of the more beginner-friendly carpeting species. Mature leaves are commonly reported in the 5-10 cm range, though stems can stretch taller under lower light. The 'Green' cultivar (Tropica 1-2-Grow tissue culture, sold by Buce Plant and KeepFishKeeping) is noted for staying fresh green even under intense lighting, in contrast with reddish varieties. Generally advised to provide a nutrient-rich substrate; root tabs tend to help establish a thick carpet. Light demand is commonly described as medium to high - it will survive under lower light but tends to grow taller and less compact. CO2 injection is optional, but supplemental CO2 (around 20-40 mg/l in the source literature) and balanced macro/micro fertilization tend to produce the densest carpets fastest. Tolerates a broad temperature range (reported 18-28°C as the comfortable band, with wider tolerances in nature). Native across the eastern United States (from Texas and Florida north to Michigan and Massachusetts), southern Mexico, the West Indies (Cuba, Jamaica, Hispaniola), and South America south to Argentina; conservation-listed in several US states. The aquarium trade often distinguishes a 'Narrow Leaf Chain Sword' form (slender grass-like blades) from a 'Broad Leaf Chain Sword' or 'Chain Sword Broad Leaf' form with wider strap-like leaves and a fuller appearance; the broad-leaf form is sometimes sold under the older name Echinodorus quadricostatus (also called 'Dwarf Swordplant'). Several taxonomic treatments fold E. quadricostatus into the broadly variable H. tenellum / H. bolivianum complex, and the trade treats them as cultivar-grade variants of the same chain-sword carpeting plant - so they are recorded here as a single entry pending firmer taxonomic resolution. The broad-leaf form is reported tolerant of a similar 20-29°C range, prefers moderate to bright light, benefits from CO2 in high-light tanks, and propagates by runners in identical fashion. Primary source (Buce Plant product page) returned HTTP 404 at access time; the assigned secondary (KeepFishKeeping) was promoted to primary and water-parameter and propagation specifics were cross-verified against Flowgrow's species database and Wikipedia.
breedingNotes: Propagates readily by sending out runners (stolons) that produce new daughter rosettes along the substrate, forming a chain - hence the common name 'Pygmy Chain Sword'. Has been reported to flower when grown emersed.

primary.name: KeepFishKeeping
primary.notes: Promoted to primary after the assigned Buce Plant primary URL (https://buceplant.com/products/helanthium-tenellum-green-tissue-culture-aquatic-farmer) returned HTTP 404.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temp 18-28°C optimal, pH 5-7.5, kH 0-14 dKH), CO2 (20-40 mg/l), height (6-10 cm), growth rate (fast), propagation (runners), and foreground placement.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Alismataceae / Alismatales), former scientific name Echinodorus tenellus, native range across the eastern US, southern Mexico, West Indies, Central and South America to Argentina, and propagation by runners.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Source for the 'Chain Sword Broad Leaf' trade variant (merged into this entry per playbook 4c). Page URL slugs the species as Echinodorus tenellus but the product description identifies it as Echinodorus quadricostatus (Dwarf Swordplant), distinguished from the 'Narrow Leaf variety' by wider strap-like leaves. Confirmed care: temperature 68-84°F (20-29°C), moderate-bright lighting, CO2 optional/beneficial, foreground placement, runner propagation, nutrient-rich substrate. Native range stated as Brazil. Taxonomic relationship between E. quadricostatus and H. tenellum is unresolved in cited sources - merged here as a trade-level variant pending firmer authority.
